Because they just didn't want any more people coming in because they were out of TVs. It's over. We're out. Don't bother us. We've sold all the 52-inch HD TVs we can get our hands on before the Super Bowl. We got no more. We can't get any more. Don't even bother coming in. I don't live in Beverly Hills. I live in a very modest community in Ohio. There's money out there flowing like it's never been flowing. But it flows to the people who decide to take it and to put in motion the factors that cause it to come to them. So the first thing is all about decision. So how much you want? How fast do you want to get it? Why not go from zero to a million instead of zero to 100,000? Why not double the business instead of increase the business incrementally by 10% a year? You just decide to do it. My favorite Iacocas story is the story of the convertible where Chrysler is belly up as they are now again. So they're on the verge of permanently going south. There's no money. They're under all kinds of pressure. He's in the midst of a turnaround. He's trying to raise money, borrow money. He's got all kinds of problems going on. There's no R&D going on. He's on the factory floor and three guys come over and say, that car would make a great convertible. And there's no convertibles being sold. No American made convertibles on the road. In case you didn't know, at Chrysler put him back on. Three guys say that car would make a great convertible. And Iacocas says, get a blow torch, cut the roof off. Let's get in and drive around, see if girls are interested in it. If there are, we'll make it. Now, there's not, you probably couldn't find another auto executive in the history of the auto industry who would do what he did. Especially under the conditions that he was under. Everybody else would organize committees, get the R&D guys in, do research, talk to the lawyers, what's going to be our litigation risk of convertibles. It would be like a three-year development project. And by the time they got the thing on the road, if they got it on the road, the company would be bankrupt or there would be 50 other convertibles on the road. No, he just said, let's go find out if, when we stop at traffic lights, girls look at us. And if they do, then we know guys will buy them. And they did. So after they took turns driving it around for a few days, then he called everybody off us and said, let's make them. No R&D, no, let's make them. That's decision. And most people like never really make decisions about these issues, who they really want to be, who are they, whoever they turned out to be. Not who they decided to be. What are they doing? They're doing some composite of what they turned out to be doing. If you ask most people why they're doing what they're doing, well, it's what I went to school to do or it's what dad did or, you know, I don't know how to do anything else. I hear that a lot, by the way, from ordinary people. This is all I know how to do. This is what you'll hear from all the auto workers who are losing their jobs. For 20 years, everybody's known they're going to lose their jobs. They've had 20 years to learn a new job. This is all I know how to do. Well, how did you get to be able to do this? I learned how to do it. Well, seemingly, if you learn how to do this, you could learn how to do something else, don't you think? I mean, so the first thing that starts money moving towards you is decision. It's making these kinds of decisions as clearly as you can. Another way to say it is clarity attracts money. Cloudiness, fuzziness does not attract money. So money goes where there is clear thought. There's an article in the... What's the local magazine called? Washington... Whatever it is that you get. What is it? Washingtonian? Washingtonian? How ever you say it. It's a local city, you know, high-end magazine. There's an article in there in this issue about Steve Case, the guy that originally started AOL, who's got the new WIS-BANG health thing that he's doing. It's an article worth reading because he's very, very clear about what he intends to do. And so consequently, I'd invest if there was a place to invest at the moment because of his clarity.
